23.40: What follows will be an everlasting shame — a lasting, unforgettable disgrace branded into your memory and into the memory of all who come after.

24.1: Yahweh gave me a vision: two baskets of figs set out before the temple. This was after Nebuchadnezzar had deported Jeconiah, son of Jehoiakim, Judah's king, together with Judah's officials, its craftsmen and metalworkers — all carried off to Babylon.
24.2: The first basket held beautiful figs — premium, early-season fruit. The second held figs so spoiled and rotten they were utterly inedible.
24.3: Then Yahweh asked me, 'What do you see, Jeremiah?' I said, 'Figs — the good ones are excellent, the bad ones are appalling, too rotten to eat.'
24.4: Then Yahweh's word came to me:
24.5: This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says: Like these good figs, I will treat well the exiles of Judah whom I sent out of this place to Babylonia. Yes — for their good.
24.6: My eye will be on them for their good — I will bring them back to this land. I will build them up and not tear them down. I will plant them and never pull them out.
24.7: And I will give them a heart to know me — to truly know that I am Yahweh. They will be my people and I will be their God, because they will come back to me with their whole heart.
24.8: But the rotten figs — the ones too spoiled to eat — that is what I will make of Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, what remains of Jerusalem still in the land, and those who have fled to Egypt. This is Yahweh's word.
24.9: I will make them a thing of horror throughout every kingdom on earth — a reproach and a proverb, a taunt and a curse, in every land where I scatter them.
24.10: And sword, starvation, and plague — I will unleash all three until they are utterly consumed, driven from the land I gave them and their ancestors.

25.1: This is the word Yahweh gave to Jeremiah about all the people of Judah — in the fourth year of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, which was the first year of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon.
25.2: Jeremiah the prophet addressed it to all the people of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem:
25.3: For twenty-three years — from the thirteenth year of Josiah son of Amon until this very day — the word of Yahweh has been coming to me. I have been speaking it to you, rising early, pressing it upon you. But you have not listened.
25.4: Yahweh sent you prophet after prophet — his servants, sent urgently and repeatedly. But you would not listen. You would not even turn your ear toward them.
25.5: The message was always the same: Turn — every one of you — from your evil path, from the wrong you are doing. Live in the land Yahweh gave you and your ancestors, the land meant to be yours forever.
25.6: Do not run after other gods to serve them and bow down to them. Do not goad me to anger with the things your hands have made. Turn, and I will not harm you.
25.7: But you did not listen, says Yahweh. You kept provoking me with your idols — and the harm falls on you.
25.8: So Yahweh of hosts says: Because you have not obeyed —
25.9: I am calling up every clan from the north, says Yahweh — and I am calling up Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, my servant. I will bring them against this land, its people, and all the nations around it. I will place them under the ban of destruction and make them a horror, a contempt, a permanent wasteland.
25.10: I will take away every sound of joy — the laughter, the celebration, the voices of bride and bridegroom, the grind of millstones in the morning, the lamp-light at night. All of it silenced.
25.11: This whole land will become a wasteland, and all these nations will be subject to the king of Babylon for seventy years — a bounded, finite judgment, not an open-ended destruction.
25.12: But when those seventy years are complete, I will call Babylon to account — king, nation, land of the Chaldeans — for their own guilt. The oppressor of nations will itself be made a ruin forever.
25.13: Every word I have spoken against Babylon I will bring to pass — everything written in this book, all that Jeremiah has prophesied against the nations.
25.14: Many nations and great kings will make Babylon serve them. And I will repay Babylon exactly according to what they have done — deeds for deeds, hand for hand.
25.15: Yahweh, God of Israel, said to me: Take this cup from my hand — the cup of the wine of my wrath — and make every nation I send you to drink from it.
25.16: They will drink and stagger and lose their minds — driven mad by the sword I am unleashing among them.
25.17: So I took the cup from Yahweh's hand and went to every nation he sent me to, and I made them drink:
25.18: First, Jerusalem and the towns of Judah — their kings and their leaders — reduced to ruin, to scorn, to a curse that lasts to this day.
25.19: Then Pharaoh king of Egypt — his servants, his officials, all his people.
25.20: All the mixed peoples. The kings of Uz. The kings of Philistia — Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, and what is left of Ashdod.
25.22: All the kings of Tyre, all the kings of Sidon, and every king on the islands and coastlands across the sea.
25.23: Dedan, Tema, Buz — all the desert tribes who crop their hair at the temples.
25.24: All the kings of Arabia. Every king of the mixed peoples scattered through the open desert.
25.25: The kings of Zimri. The kings of Elam. The kings of Media.
25.26: All the kings of the north — near and far, one after another — every kingdom on the face of the earth. And at the end of the list: the king of Sheshach will drink too. Sheshach is the cipher name for Babylon.
25.27: Then say to them: Yahweh of hosts, God of Israel, says this — Drink. Get drunk and retch. Fall down and do not get up, because of the sword I am sending among you.
25.28: If they refuse to take the cup from your hand, say to them: Yahweh of hosts says — You will drink. There is no refusal.
25.29: I am starting with the city that carries my own name — and if I am doing that to Jerusalem, do you imagine you will go free? You will not. I am summoning the sword against every inhabitant of the earth. Yahweh of hosts has declared it.
25.30: Prophesy all of this against them. Say: Yahweh roars from on high. From his holy dwelling his voice thunders. He roars over his flock like a lion, and shouts against all the inhabitants of the earth the way a grape-treader shouts at harvest — exultant, relentless, the weight of the harvest underfoot.
25.31: The sound reaches the ends of the earth — because Yahweh has filed his indictment against the nations. He is putting all flesh on trial, and the guilty he is giving over to the sword.
25.32: Yahweh of hosts says: Calamity is rolling outward from nation to nation, and a great whirlwind is rising from the ends of the earth.
25.33: The dead that day will be strewn from one end of the earth to the other. No lament will be sung for them. No one will gather them. No one will bury them. They will lie like dung on the face of the ground.
25.34: Wail, shepherds — cry out! Roll in the ashes, you who lead the flock! The days of your slaughter have come and cannot be deferred. You will fall and shatter like a precious jar — the same image Jeremiah enacted at Tophet.
25.35: There is no escape for the shepherds, no way out for the leaders of the flock.
25.36: Listen: the cry of the shepherds, the wail of those who led the flock. Yahweh is destroying their pastures.
25.37: Every quiet pasture is laid waste by the heat of Yahweh's burning anger.
25.38: He has left his lair like a lion — because their land has been turned to ruin by the oppressor's sword and by the blazing force of his wrath.

26.1: At the very start of Jehoiakim son of Josiah's reign as king of Judah, this word came from Yahweh:
26.2: Yahweh says: Stand in the court of the temple. Speak to all the people who come from Judah's towns to worship. Say everything I command — every word, nothing withheld.
26.3: Perhaps they will listen. Perhaps they will turn from their evil ways, and I can turn from the judgment I have planned against them because of what they have done.
26.4: Say to them: Yahweh says — If you will not listen to me, if you will not walk by the Torah I have set before you,
26.5: and if you will not hear my servants the prophets, whom I keep sending persistently — you who have never listened —
26.6: then I will make this house what Shiloh became — and I will make this city a curse word in the mouth of every nation on earth.
26.7: The priests, the prophets, and all the people heard Jeremiah deliver that message in the temple.
26.8: The moment Jeremiah finished the full message Yahweh had commanded him to deliver, the priests and the prophets and all the people grabbed him. You are going to die! they said.
26.9: How dare you prophesy in Yahweh's name and say this house will become like Shiloh, that this city will lie empty with no one in it! All the people closed in around Jeremiah in the temple court.
26.10: When the officials of Judah heard what was going on, they came up from the royal palace to the temple and sat down at the New Gate — the place of public judgment.
26.11: The priests and the prophets pressed their case to the officials and all the people: This man deserves death. You heard it yourselves — he prophesied against this city.
26.12: Jeremiah answered the officials and all the people: Yahweh sent me. He sent me to prophesy against this house and against this city — every word you heard.
26.13: Now then — change your ways and change your deeds, and listen to the voice of Yahweh your God. If you do, Yahweh will turn from the disaster he has decreed against you.
26.14: As for me — I am in your hands. Do whatever you think is right.
26.15: But know this for certain: if you kill me, you are loading innocent blood onto yourselves, onto this city, onto everyone in it. Yahweh truly sent me to speak every one of these words to you.
26.16: Then the officials and all the people rendered their verdict to the priests and prophets: This man is not guilty of a capital offense. He spoke to us in the name of Yahweh our God.
26.17: Then some of the elders of the land rose to address the full assembly:
26.18: Micah of Moresheth prophesied during Hezekiah's reign. He said to all the people of Judah: Yahweh of hosts says — Zion will be plowed under like a field. Jerusalem will be a heap of stones. The temple mount will be a thicket.
26.19: Did Hezekiah and all Judah execute Micah for saying that? No. Hezekiah feared Yahweh and pleaded with him, and Yahweh relented from the disaster he had announced. If we kill this man, we bring tremendous evil down on ourselves.
26.20: But there was another man who prophesied in Yahweh's name — Urijah son of Shemaiah from Kiriath-jearim. He preached the same message against this city and this land.
26.21: When King Jehoiakim heard him — along with all his officers and warriors — the king wanted him dead. Urijah found out and fled in fear to Egypt.
26.22: But Jehoiakim sent a team to Egypt — Elnathan son of Achbor and others with him.
26.23: They brought Urijah back from Egypt. Jehoiakim had him put to the sword. His body was thrown into the graves of the common people — not a royal burial, not even a named grave.
26.24: Ahikam son of Shaphan stood with Jeremiah, and his protection kept Jeremiah out of the hands of those who wanted him dead.

27.1: At the start of Zedekiah son of Josiah's reign as king of Judah, this word came to Jeremiah from Yahweh. (The Hebrew text reads 'Jehoiakim' — almost certainly a scribal slip; the chapter plainly addresses Zedekiah throughout.)
27.2: Yahweh told me: Make a yoke — crossbars and straps — and put it on your neck.
27.3: Use the envoys who have come to Jerusalem to meet with Zedekiah — send word to the king of Edom, the king of Moab, the king of the Ammonites, the king of Tyre, and the king of Sidon.
27.4: This is what to deliver to their kings: Yahweh of hosts, God of Israel, says this. Tell your masters:
27.5: I made the earth. I made every person and animal on it — by my great power, with my outstretched arm. And I give it to whoever I choose. The arm that delivered Israel from Egypt now gives the world to Babylon.
27.6: And right now, I am giving all these lands into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon — my servant. I am even putting the wild animals under his authority.
27.7: All nations will serve him — him, his son, his grandson — until the moment of reckoning comes for his own land. Then many nations and great kings will put Babylon under the yoke.
27.8: But any nation that refuses to serve Nebuchadnezzar, that will not put its neck under Babylon's yoke — I will punish it with sword, famine, and plague, says Yahweh, until I have consumed it by his hand.
27.9: Do not listen to your prophets, your diviners, your dreamers, your soothsayers, your sorcerers — the ones telling you that you will not have to serve the king of Babylon.
27.10: They are feeding you lies. The outcome will be that you are torn from your land — I will drive you out, and you will be destroyed.
27.11: But the nation that puts its neck under Babylon's yoke and serves — that nation I will leave in its own land to farm and inhabit. Yahweh's word.
27.12: I spoke the same message directly to Zedekiah king of Judah: Put your neck under Babylon's yoke. Serve him and his people. Live.
27.13: Why die by sword, famine, and plague — the exact fate Yahweh has announced for every nation that refuses to submit to Babylon?
27.14: Do not listen to the prophets who say you will not serve Babylon. They are lying to you.
27.15: I did not send them, says Yahweh. They prophesy falsely in my name. The outcome will be that I drive you out and you are destroyed — you and the very prophets you are listening to.
27.16: Then I addressed the priests and all the people: Yahweh says — Do not listen to your prophets who keep saying the temple vessels will be brought back from Babylon any day now. It is a lie.
27.17: Do not listen to them. Serve Babylon and live. Why would you let this city be turned into a ruin?
27.18: If they are truly prophets, if Yahweh's word is actually with them — let them intercede. Let them pray to Yahweh of hosts that the vessels still in the temple, in the palace, in Jerusalem not be taken to Babylon. That would be worth praying about.
27.19: Yahweh of hosts has spoken about the great bronze pillars, the bronze sea, the movable stands, and all the remaining vessels still in this city —
27.20: the vessels Nebuchadnezzar did not take when he exiled Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim to Babylon, along with all the nobility of Judah and Jerusalem —
27.21: Yahweh of hosts, God of Israel, says this about those remaining vessels in the temple and the palace and throughout Jerusalem:
27.22: They will be carried to Babylon. They will stay there until the day I come for them, says Yahweh. Then I will bring them back and restore them to this place.

28.1: It happened in the fourth year of Zedekiah's reign over Judah, in the fifth month, that Hananiah son of Azzur, a prophet from Gibeon, confronted me in Yahweh's temple. The priests and the whole gathered people were listening when he spoke:
28.2: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, declares: I have shattered the yoke of Babylon's king.
28.3: Within two full years I will return every vessel of Yahweh's temple to this very place — everything Nebuchadnezzar took and hauled off to Babylon.
28.4: And Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim, Judah's king, along with all the Judahite exiles in Babylon — I will bring them all home. The yoke of Babylon's king will be broken. Yahweh's word.
28.5: Then Jeremiah, prophet that he was, answered Hananiah, prophet that he was — in front of the priests and the whole gathered assembly, right there in Yahweh's temple:
28.6: Yes — amen! Would that Yahweh would do exactly what you say! Would that he fulfilled every word you have prophesied and brought the temple vessels and all the exiles home from Babylon!
28.7: But hear me out — there is a word I must speak in your ears and in the ears of every person standing here:
28.8: Every prophet who came before us — every authentic voice from antiquity — brought oracles of war, disaster, and plague against nations and kingdoms. That is the default register of true prophecy.
28.9: A prophet who promises peace? Fine — but let's wait and see. When his word actually comes to pass, that is when we will know that Yahweh genuinely sent him.
28.10: At that, Hananiah the prophet reached over, lifted the yoke from Jeremiah's neck, and broke it in pieces.
28.11: Hananiah proclaimed to the whole crowd: 'This is what Yahweh says: This is exactly how I will break Nebuchadnezzar's yoke from the neck of every nation — within two full years!' And Jeremiah simply turned and walked away.
28.12: After Hananiah had broken the yoke bar from Jeremiah's neck, Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah:
28.13: Go back and tell Hananiah: This is what Yahweh says — You smashed wooden yokes, but you have forged iron yokes to take their place.
28.14: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: I have fitted an iron yoke to the neck of all these nations — they will serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and they have no choice. I have even placed the wild animals under his hand.
28.15: So Jeremiah said to Hananiah directly: Listen carefully, Hananiah. Yahweh never sent you. What you are doing is making this people stake their lives on a lie.
28.16: So this is what Yahweh says: I am removing you from the land of the living. You will die this year, because you spoke in Yahweh's name to incite rebellion against him.
28.17: Hananiah the prophet died that very year — in the seventh month.

29.1: This is the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the elders still living among the exiles — to the priests, the prophets, and all the people Nebuchadnezzar had carried off from Jerusalem to Babylon.
29.2: This came after King Jeconiah, the queen mother, the court officials, the princes of Judah and Jerusalem, and all the craftsmen and metalworkers had been taken from Jerusalem.
29.3: The letter traveled by the hand of Elasah son of Shaphan and Gemariah son of Hilkiah — the envoys King Zedekiah was dispatching to Nebuchadnezzar in Babylon. This is what it said:
29.4: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says to all of you whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon:
29.5: Build houses — put down roots in them. Plant gardens — eat what they yield.
29.6: Marry, and raise children. Find wives for your sons and give your daughters to husbands, so that your grandchildren fill Babylon too. Grow in number — do not shrink.
29.7: Seek the shalom of the city where I have placed you in exile — pray to Yahweh for it — because when that city flourishes, you flourish with it.
29.8: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: Do not let the prophets and fortune-tellers in your community deceive you. Do not pay attention to the dreams they conjure.
29.9: They are speaking lies and calling it my word. I never commissioned a single one of them. Yahweh says so.
29.10: This is what Yahweh says: When Babylon's seventy years are over, I will act on your behalf and make good on my promise — I will bring you back to this very place.
29.11: I know exactly what I have planned for you — Yahweh declares it. Plans for your flourishing, not your ruin. Plans to give you a real future, a genuine hope.
29.12: Then you will call out to me. You will come to me and pray — and I will hear every word.
29.13: You will search for me — and you will find me — when you seek with your whole heart, not halfway.
29.14: I will be found — Yahweh declares it. I will turn your captivity around and gather you from every nation and every land where I drove you. Yahweh's word: I will bring you home to the very place from which you were exiled.
29.15: But you are saying: 'Yahweh has given us prophets even here in Babylon.' —
29.16: Then hear what Yahweh says about the king now sitting on David's throne and all the people still in Jerusalem — your own kin who were not carried off into exile with you:
29.17: Yahweh of hosts says this: I am sending sword, famine, and plague against them — making them like inedible, rotten figs, repulsive to the core.
29.18: I will hunt them down with sword, starvation, and plague, and scatter them as a curse and a horror and an object of hissing among all the kingdoms of the earth — a byword of ruin in every nation where I drive them.
29.19: All of this because they refused to hear my words — Yahweh declares it — the words I sent them through my servants the prophets, urgently, again and again. They would not listen.
29.20: So hear the word of Yahweh — all of you whom I have sent from Jerusalem into exile in Babylon:
29.21: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says about Ahab son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah son of Maaseiah — those two who peddle lies in my name: I am handing them over to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, who will execute them before your eyes.
29.22: Their fate will become a curse-formula on the lips of every Judean exile in Babylon: 'May Yahweh make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom Babylon's king roasted alive.'
29.23: What they did in Israel is obscene — adultery with their neighbors' wives, and then fabricating oracles and calling them mine, words I never authorized. I know every detail. I am the witness. Yahweh says so.
29.24: And you are to send a word to Shemaiah the Nehelamite — this message:
29.25: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: You have sent letters under your own signature to all the people in Jerusalem — including Zephaniah son of Maaseiah the priest and the entire priesthood — and in those letters you wrote:
29.26: Yahweh has placed you as chief priest in Jehoiada's stead, with authority over the temple. Any madman who sets himself up as a prophet is to be arrested, put in the stocks, and locked in an iron collar.
29.27: So why have you not silenced Jeremiah of Anathoth, who keeps playing the prophet in your midst?
29.28: Because — consider — he actually sent word to us in Babylon: 'Your exile is going to be a long one. Build houses, settle in, plant gardens, eat what they produce.' That is what he told us!
29.29: Zephaniah the priest read the letter aloud to Jeremiah the prophet.
29.30: Then Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah:
29.31: Send this message to all the exiles: Yahweh speaks concerning Shemaiah the Nehelamite. Because he prophesied to you without any commission from me, and led you to build your lives on a lie —
29.32: this is what Yahweh says: I will bring punishment on Shemaiah the Nehelamite and his entire line. None of his descendants will live to see the day when I restore my people — Yahweh declares it — because he spent his life inciting rebellion against Yahweh.

30.1: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from Yahweh:
30.2: This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says: Write down every word I have spoken to you — record it all in a scroll.
30.3: Days are coming — Yahweh declares it — when I will turn the captivity of my people Israel and Judah around and bring them back to the land I gave their ancestors. It will be theirs again.
30.4: These are Yahweh's words for Israel and for Judah.
30.5: This is what Yahweh says: The sound that reaches us is sheer terror — panic, not peace.
30.6: Ask yourself, look carefully: do men give birth? Then why does every man grip his sides like a woman in the agony of labor? Why has every face drained to ash?
30.7: What a day that will be — unmatched in its horror! It is the hour of Jacob's agony. Yet he will be rescued from it.
30.8: On that day — Yahweh of hosts declares it — I will shatter the yoke on your neck and tear your chains apart. Never again will a foreign master own you.
30.9: Instead they will serve Yahweh their God and David their king — the one I will raise up for them.
30.10: So do not be afraid, Jacob my servant — Yahweh declares it. Do not panic, Israel. I am bringing you home from a great distance, your children from the land of their exile. Jacob will come back and live in peace and security — nothing will make him tremble.
30.11: I am with you — Yahweh declares it — to deliver you. Though I bring every nation where I scattered you to complete ruin, I will not bring you to complete ruin. I will correct you, and the correction will be just — but I will not simply let you off.
30.12: This is what Yahweh says: Your wound is past all human healing. Your injury is beyond treatment.
30.13: No advocate will rise to argue your case. No physician has medicine for this wound. There is no recovery to be had from any human source.
30.14: Every ally you relied on has forgotten you and stopped caring whether you live or die. Because I myself struck you as an enemy strikes — with the severity of a merciless adversary — because your guilt is enormous and your sins have piled up beyond measure.
30.15: Why do you keep crying out about your suffering? Your pain is incurable — and it is deserved. It is because your guilt is so great and your sins have gone on so long that I have brought all this on you.
30.16: But this is where it turns: everyone who consumed you will themselves be consumed. All your enemies will go into their own exile. Those who stripped you bare will be stripped. Those who preyed on you — I will make them the prey.
30.17: I will restore you to health and close every wound — Yahweh declares it. Why? Because they said you were an outcast, said that nobody wants Zion, nobody comes looking for her. I am coming.
30.18: This is what Yahweh says: I am turning Jacob's exile around, showing compassion on every home that now lies empty. The city will rise again on its ancient mound, and the royal palace will stand where it stood before.
30.19: From those rebuilt streets will come songs of praise and the sound of celebration. I will multiply their numbers — they will not be a remnant rump. I will give them dignity — they will not be a forgotten footnote among the nations.
30.20: Their children will flourish as they did in the old days. Their community will stand firm before me. And every nation that oppresses them — I will deal with them.
30.21: Their ruler will be one of their own — no foreign governor placed over them from outside. Their leader will rise from among themselves. I will draw him close, and he will have access to me. For who would ever presume on their own to approach me? Yahweh asks.
30.22: You will be my people. I will be your God.
30.23: Look — the storm of Yahweh is breaking out! His fury sweeps forward like a howling whirlwind, crashing down on the heads of the wicked.
30.24: Yahweh's fierce anger will not relent until every intention of his heart is executed and complete. In the days that are coming — then you will understand it.

7.1: This is the word that came from Yahweh to Jeremiah:
7.2: Take your stand at the gate of Yahweh's house and declare this word there: 'Hear the word of Yahweh, all you of Judah who pass through these gates to worship him.'
7.3: Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel, says this: Truly reform your ways and your deeds — and I will let you dwell in this land.
7.4: Stop trusting the lie — 'The temple of Yahweh! The temple of Yahweh! The temple of Yahweh!' — as if the building itself guaranteed your safety.
7.5: Only if you truly reform your conduct — if you genuinely practice justice between one person and another —
7.6: if you stop exploiting the immigrant, the orphan, and the widow — stop shedding innocent blood in this place — stop chasing other gods to your own destruction —
7.7: then I will let you remain in this land — the land I gave your ancestors as an everlasting possession.
7.8: But the truth is this: you are placing your trust in a lie — words that accomplish nothing.
7.9: You steal, you murder, you commit adultery, you swear false oaths, you burn offerings to Baal, you run after gods you have never even known —
7.10: and then walk in here and stand before me in this house that bears my name, crying, 'We are safe now!' — and then turn right back to all these very abominations?
7.11: Has this house — the house that bears my name — become a criminals' hideout in your eyes? Hear me: I have seen it with my own eyes. Yahweh declares it.
7.12: Go to Shiloh — to the place where I first made my name dwell — and see with your own eyes what I did to it because of the evil of my people Israel.
7.13: Now you have done all these things — Yahweh declares. I rose early and spoke, again and again. I called out to you. You would not listen. You would not answer.
7.14: So I will do to this house — the house that carries my name, the house you have trusted as your guarantee — what I did to Shiloh. I gave this place to you and your ancestors; I can take it back.
7.15: I will hurl you out of my sight, the same way I expelled all your northern kin — every last descendant of Ephraim.
7.16: And you, Jeremiah — do not pray for this people. Do not raise a cry or a plea on their behalf. Do not intercede with me. I will not listen.
7.17: Don't you see it — what they are doing throughout Judah's towns and in every street of Jerusalem?
7.18: Children gather the fuel, fathers light the fire, women knead the dough — all to bake offerings for the queen of heaven. And they pour out libations to foreign gods, as if they want to goad me into fury.
7.19: Is it me they are provoking? — Yahweh asks. No — they are provoking themselves, heaping shame upon their own heads.
7.20: Therefore this is what the Lord GOD declares: My anger and my fury are about to be poured out on this place — on human and animal alike, on the trees of the field and the produce of the land. It will burn, and no one will quench it.
7.21: Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel, says this: Pile your burnt offerings onto your peace offerings and eat all the meat yourselves.
7.22: Because on the day I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, burnt offerings were not my primary word to them. That ritual was not the foundational command.
7.23: This was my foundational command: 'Listen to my voice. I will be your God; you will be my people. Walk in every way I have shown you — and it will go well with you.'
7.24: But they would not listen. They refused to pay attention. They followed the schemes of their own corrupt hearts — walking backward, away from me, not toward me.
7.25: From the day your ancestors left Egypt until this very day, I have sent my servants the prophets — day after day, rising early to send them. And I have never stopped.
7.26: Still they would not listen. Still they refused to pay attention — making their necks rigid against me. And with each generation they grew worse than those before them.
7.27: Speak all these words to them — they will not hear you. Call out to them — they will not answer. This is the mission Yahweh has given you.
7.28: Then announce the verdict: 'This is a nation that would not obey Yahweh their God, would not accept correction. Truth has perished — it has been cut out of their mouths entirely.'
7.29: Shave your head and cast the hair to the wind. Lift a funeral wail on the bare hilltops — for Yahweh has rejected and abandoned this generation on whom his wrath has fallen.
7.30: The people of Judah have done what is evil in my sight — Yahweh declares. They placed their vile idols in the very house that bears my name, polluting it to the core.
7.31: They built the high places of Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom and burned their sons and daughters in the fire — something I never commanded, something that never even crossed my mind.
7.32: Therefore, days are coming — Yahweh declares — when it will no longer be called Topheth, or the Valley of Ben Hinnom. It will be called the Valley of Slaughter. They will bury the bodies in Topheth until there is no more room.
7.33: The bodies of this people will lie as carrion for the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth — and no one will be left to drive them off.
7.34: I will silence every sound of joy and celebration from the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — the laughter of revelers, the songs of bridegroom and bride. The land will become a desolation.

8.1: At that time — Yahweh declares — they will drag the bones out of their graves: the bones of Judah's kings, their officials, the priests, the prophets, and the ordinary citizens of Jerusalem.
8.2: Those bones will be spread out like dung before the sun, the moon, and all the starry host — the very gods they loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshipped. No one will gather them for burial. They will lie like refuse on the open ground.
8.3: Every survivor of this wicked family — scattered to every place where I have driven them — will choose death over life. Yahweh of Armies declares it.
8.4: Say this to them: Here is what Yahweh says: When a man falls down, doesn't he get up? When someone wanders off the path, doesn't he turn back?
8.5: Then why has this people — Jerusalem — turned away in endless, habitual apostasy? They grip their delusion tight; they will not turn back.
8.6: I listened carefully — but no one spoke honestly. No one stopped and asked, 'What have I done?' Everyone plunges ahead on their own course, like a warhorse charging into battle with no thought of turning.
8.7: Even the stork knows its season; the dove, the swallow, the crane — all observe their migration times by instinct. But my own people don't even know what Yahweh requires of them.
8.8: You say, 'We are wise — we have Yahweh's law.' But look what the scribes' deceptive pens have done with it: they have turned the law itself into a lie.
8.9: The wise will be shamed; they will be caught out and trapped. They threw away the word of Yahweh — so where is their wisdom now? It has come to nothing.
8.10: So I will hand their wives over to others, their fields to new masters — because every one of them, from least to greatest, is driven by greed. Prophet and priest alike — every last one of them is a liar.
8.11: They treat my people's gaping wound as if it were a scratch. 'All is well, all is well!' — but nothing is well.
8.12: Did they feel any shame when they did these vile things? Not at all — they had forgotten how to blush. So they will fall with the fallen; when the hour of reckoning arrives, they will go down with the rest. Yahweh says it.
8.13: I will strip away what I gave them — Yahweh declares. No grapes left on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, even the leaves withered and gone. Everything I bestowed on them — taken back.
8.14: Why are we sitting still? Gather together and flee to the fortified cities — let us at least die there! For Yahweh our God has sentenced us, given us poison to drink. This is what our sin against Yahweh has brought.
8.15: We waited for peace — nothing came. We hoped for healing — and terror arrived in its place.
8.16: From Dan comes the snorting of their warhorses; the neighing of their stallions makes the whole land tremble. They are coming to devour the land — everything in it — every city and every person.
8.17: For I am sending venomous snakes among you — serpents no charmer can control — and they will bite you. Yahweh declares it.
8.18: My sorrow is past all mending — my heart collapses within me.
8.19: I hear it — the anguished cry of my people rising from a far country: 'Has Yahweh abandoned Zion? Is her King no longer there?' But the divine answer breaks in: 'Why did they provoke me with their carved images, their worthless foreign gods?'
8.20: The harvest has come and gone; summer has ended — and still we are not saved.
8.21: My people are shattered — and I am shattered with them. Grief overwhelms me; horror has seized me by the heart.
8.22: Is there no healing resin in Gilead? Is there no physician there? The medicine exists — the healer exists — then why, why has my people's wound not healed?

9.1: If only my head were a spring and my eyes an endless fountain of tears — I would weep day and night for the fallen of my people.
9.2: If only I could find a desert way-station — I would abandon my people and flee from them. They are all covenant-breakers, a congregation of betrayers.
9.3: They string their tongue like a bow, aimed with lies. Falsehood, not faithfulness, has grown dominant in this land. They lurch from one evil to the next — and still will not know me. Yahweh declares it.
9.4: Don't trust your neighbor; don't rely on any kinsman — for every brother is out to trip you up, and every neighbor makes a living by slander.
9.5: They deceive one another; no one speaks the truth. They have trained their tongues for lying — wearing themselves out in the practice of sin.
9.6: You dwell inside a world of deception; through deception they have chosen not to know me. Yahweh declares it.
9.7: Therefore Yahweh of Armies says this: I will put them through the refiner's fire and test them. What else can I do with my people?
9.8: Their tongue is a deadly arrow shot with deception. They speak peace to your face — but inside they are already setting the trap.
9.9: Shall I not call them to account for this? — Yahweh demands. Shall I not exact justice from a nation that behaves this way?
9.10: I lift up weeping and wailing for the mountains, a funeral lament for the wilderness pastures — they are scorched and empty. No one passes through; no cattle low; the birds have taken flight and the wild animals have fled. Nothing remains.
9.11: I will turn Jerusalem into a rubble heap, a den for jackals. The cities of Judah I will make a wasteland, emptied of every living soul.
9.12: Who is wise enough to grasp this? Who has heard from Yahweh and can interpret it for others? Why has the land been devastated, turned into a desert that no one dares cross?
9.13: Yahweh answers: Because they abandoned my law — the instruction I set before them. They would not listen to my voice; they would not live by it.
9.14: Instead they followed the stubbornness of their own hearts, pursuing the Baals — exactly as their fathers had taught them.
9.15: Therefore Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel, says this: I am about to feed this people on bitter wormwood and make them drink toxic water.
9.16: I will scatter them among nations they have never known — not they nor their ancestors. And I will send the sword chasing after them until I have consumed every last one.
9.17: Yahweh of Armies says: Think hard about this — and send for the mourning women. Call for the most skilled among them.
9.18: Let them come quickly and lift up the funeral wail over us — so that our eyes run with tears and our eyelids pour down with weeping.
9.19: For the sound of wailing is already rising from Zion: 'We are ruined! We are utterly shamed — because we were driven from the land, because our homes have been reduced to rubble.'
9.20: Women — hear the word of Yahweh! Let the word from his mouth reach your ears. Teach your daughters this lament; teach one another this song of mourning.
9.21: For death has climbed through our windows; it has entered our palaces — cutting down children in the streets, young men in the open squares.
9.22: This is what Yahweh declares: 'Bodies will fall like dung across the open field — like the cut stalks a reaper leaves behind, with no one to come and collect them.'
9.23: This is what Yahweh says: 'Don't let the wise crow about their wisdom. Don't let the strong crow about their strength. Don't let the wealthy crow about their riches.
9.24: If anyone wants to boast, let them boast in this alone: that they know me and understand who I am — Yahweh, who acts with covenant faithfulness, justice, and righteousness throughout the earth. These are what I delight in. Yahweh declares it.'
9.25: The days are coming — Yahweh declares — when I will call to account all who are circumcised in body only:
9.26: Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and all the desert peoples who shave the edges of their hair — these nations are uncircumcised in body, but all the house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart. The outer sign means nothing without the inner reality.

31.1: At that time — Yahweh declares it — I will be God to every family of Israel. They will be my people.
31.2: This is what Yahweh says: The survivors of the sword found unexpected grace out in the wilderness — Israel, trudging toward rest.
31.3: From ages past Yahweh spoke to me: I have loved you with a love that has no end. That is why I have drawn you close — with covenant faithfulness that never gave up on you.
31.4: I will build you up again, Israel — you who are beloved, who have been kept. You will be rebuilt. You will pick up your tambourines and dance again with those who make merry.
31.5: Once more vineyards will go in on the hills of Samaria. Those who plant them will live to eat the fruit with their own hands.
31.6: A day is coming when watchmen on the heights of Ephraim will shout the call: Rise up! We are going up to Zion — to Yahweh our God!
31.7: This is what Yahweh says: Break into song for Jacob — rejoice! Shout it out before all the nations. Announce it, praise it, cry it out: Save your people, Yahweh! Save the remnant of Israel!
31.8: Watch — I am bringing them home from the north country, gathering them from the ends of the earth. No one is left out: blind and lame, pregnant women and those in the grip of labor — all of them coming together in one great procession.
31.9: They will come home weeping — and with their prayers still rising from their lips I will lead them. I will set them on a level road beside flowing water, where no foot will stumble. For I am Israel's father, and Ephraim is my firstborn son.
31.10: Nations — hear the word of Yahweh! Carry it to the farthest coastlands. Say it: The one who scattered Israel will gather him back. He will guard him the way a shepherd guards his flock.
31.11: Yahweh has ransomed Jacob — bought him back, liberated him from a grip too powerful for him to break.
31.12: They will stream to the heights of Zion, singing, their faces lit up by the beauty of Yahweh's generosity — grain, wine, oil, the young of flocks and herds, all of it overflowing. Their whole selves will be like a garden with deep springs, and never again will they pine away.
31.13: Young women will dance. Young men and elders will join them together. For I will transform their grief into joy — I will comfort them and trade their sorrow in for exuberance.
31.14: The priests will eat their fill of richness; my people will be satisfied with every good thing I provide. Yahweh declares it.
31.15: This is what Yahweh says: A voice is heard in Ramah — a sound of lament and bitter, bitter weeping. Rachel weeping for her children. She will not be comforted. Her children are gone.
31.16: This is what Yahweh says: Stop the weeping. No more tears. Your anguish was not wasted. Your children will come back from the land of the enemy. Yahweh has spoken.
31.17: There is a future — Yahweh declares it — and there is hope. Your children will come home to their own ground.
31.18: I have heard every word of Ephraim grieving: 'You broke me, and I was broken — like a calf that has never been under the yoke. Bring me back. Turn me around. Only you can do it — Yahweh, my God.'
31.19: After I turned away, I came to my senses — I was filled with regret. When I finally understood what I had done, I struck my thigh in shame. I was embarrassed to the core, still carrying the reproach of my foolish early years.
31.20: Is Ephraim not still my dear son? My beloved child? Even when I must speak against him, I keep thinking about him — longing for him. My whole being is moved with tenderness. I will have mercy. I will surely have mercy. Yahweh declares it.
31.21: Plant your own guideposts on the road. Mark the way you went. Fix your attention on the highway — that road you traveled when you were taken away. Now come back on it. Come home, Israel. Every town that was yours is waiting.
31.22: How long will you keep wavering, faithless Israel? Yahweh has done something unprecedented — created a new thing in the earth: a woman will seek out a man.
31.23: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: When I turn Judah's fortunes around, when the cities fill with life again, people will say to one another: Yahweh bless you — dwelling place of justice, holy mountain!
31.24: Judah and all its towns will fill with life — farmers working the land, shepherds ranging the pastures — all of them together.
31.25: I have given the exhausted what they could not find on their own. Every aching soul I have filled to the full.
31.26: Then I woke, and looked about me, and found that my sleep had been sweet.
31.27: Days are coming — Yahweh declares it — when I will plant both Israel and Judah with new life: human beings and animals spreading across the land like seed scattered from a sower's hand.
31.28: In the past I watched over them with the full force of my judgment — uprooting, tearing down, overthrowing, destroying. With that same relentless attention I will now watch over them to build and to plant. Yahweh declares it.
31.29: In those days no one will use this proverb anymore: The parents ate the sour grapes, but the children's teeth feel the bitterness.
31.30: Instead: each person bears their own sin. If you eat the sour grape, your own teeth feel it. Personal accountability — not inherited guilt.
31.31: Days are coming — Yahweh declares it — when I will establish a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah —
31.32: not the covenant I made with their ancestors the day I took them by the hand and led them out of Egypt. They broke that covenant, even though I was their husband. Yahweh declares it.
31.33: But this is the covenant I will make with the house of Israel when those days have come — Yahweh declares it: I will put my teaching inside them. I will inscribe it on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be my people.
31.34: There will be no more need to teach a fellow citizen or a sibling — 'Learn to know Yahweh' — because they will all know me already, from the smallest child to the most prominent elder. Yahweh declares it. Because I will have pardoned their wrongdoing. Their sin — I will not call it to mind again.
31.35: This is what Yahweh says — he who set the sun to light the day, who arranged the moon and stars as lamps for the night, who stirs the sea until its waves thunder — Yahweh of hosts is his name:
31.36: Only if those cosmic ordinances fail — only if they depart from before me, Yahweh declares — only then would the descendants of Israel cease to be a people before me. It will never happen.
31.37: This is what Yahweh says: Could someone measure the full expanse of heaven? Could they probe the foundations beneath the earth? If they could — only then would I reject Israel in its entirety, for everything they have done. Yahweh declares it: I will not.
31.38: Days are coming — Yahweh declares it — when the city will be rebuilt and consecrated to Yahweh, from the Tower of Hananeel all the way to the Corner Gate.
31.39: The surveyor's line will run straight to the hill of Gareb, then curve around toward Goah.
31.40: Even the valley of corpses and ash — even those defiled fields stretching to the Kidron Brook, to the corner of the Horse Gate on the east — all of it will become holy to Yahweh. Never to be torn up or torn down. Not ever again. Forever.

32.1: In the tenth year of Zedekiah king of Judah — Nebuchadnezzar's eighteenth year of reign — this word came to Jeremiah from Yahweh.
32.2: The Babylonian army was surrounding Jerusalem when all this happened. Jeremiah the prophet was under confinement in the guardhouse court inside Judah's royal palace.
32.3: Zedekiah king of Judah had locked him up with this charge: 'Why do you keep prophesying? Why do you keep saying: This is what Yahweh declares — I am handing this city to Babylon's king, and he will take it?'
32.4: And Zedekiah king of Judah will not slip away from the Chaldeans. He will be handed over personally to Babylon's king. He will stand before him face to face, eye to eye.
32.5: Zedekiah will be taken to Babylon and will stay there until the day I act on his case. Yahweh declares it: however long you fight against the Chaldeans, it will come to nothing.
32.6: Jeremiah continued his account: Yahweh's word came to me.
32.7: Your cousin Hanamel son of Shallum is going to come to you and say: Buy my field at Anathoth — you have the right of first redemption, the right of purchase.
32.8: And just as Yahweh had said, my cousin Hanamel came to me in the guardhouse courtyard. He said: 'Buy my field at Anathoth in Benjamin territory — as next of kin you have the right of redemption. It belongs to you. Buy it.' I knew then with certainty: this was Yahweh's word.
32.9: I bought the field at Anathoth from my cousin Hanamel. I weighed out the price — seventeen shekels of silver.
32.10: I signed the deed and sealed it, brought in witnesses, and weighed out the silver on the scales.
32.11: I took both copies of the deed — the sealed copy with the full legal terms inside, and the open copy for public inspection.
32.12: I handed the deed to Baruch son of Neriah son of Mahseiah, with my cousin Hanamel, all the signing witnesses, and every Judean sitting in the guardhouse court watching the whole transaction.
32.13: Right there in front of them all I instructed Baruch:
32.14: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: Take these documents — the sealed deed and the open copy — and put them in a clay jar. Seal them in so they will last for many years to come.
32.15: Because this is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: Houses, fields, and vineyards will be bought and sold again in this land.
32.16: After handing the deed to Baruch son of Neriah, I turned to Yahweh in prayer:
32.17: Ah, Lord Yahweh! You made the heavens and the earth with your great power and your outstretched arm. There is nothing — nothing — too hard for you.
32.18: You show covenant faithfulness to thousands, yet you repay the guilt of parents into the hands of their children who come after. Great God, Mighty God — Yahweh of hosts is your name.
32.19: Vast in wisdom, mighty in action. Your eyes are open to every path every human being walks, and you give each one what their life has earned — the full fruit of what they have done.
32.20: You performed signs and wonders in Egypt, and that mighty work has reverberated through Israel and among all humanity right up to this day. You made your name known — and it remains known.
32.21: You brought Israel your people out of Egypt — with signs and wonders, with a powerful hand and an outstretched arm, with tremendous and awe-inspiring force.
32.22: You gave them this very land, the land you swore to their ancestors — a land flowing with milk and honey.
32.23: They entered and took possession — and then did none of what you commanded. No obedience to your voice, no walking in your law, nothing from the entire list of what you required. So you brought every one of these disasters on them.
32.24: The siege ramps are up against the city walls. The city is being handed to the Chaldeans attacking it — sword, famine, and plague doing their work. Everything you said has come true. You yourself are watching it happen right now.
32.25: And yet you, Lord Yahweh, told me to buy the field. Pay for it. Get witnesses. Even while the city is being surrendered to the Chaldeans.
32.26: Then Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah.
32.27: I am Yahweh — God of all flesh. Is there anything too difficult for me?
32.28: Therefore this is what Yahweh says: I am handing this city over — to the Chaldeans and to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon. He will take it.
32.29: The Chaldeans attacking this city will come and torch it — the city and all the houses where incense burned to Baal on the rooftops and libations were poured out to foreign gods, all of it done deliberately to provoke me.
32.30: Because both Israel and Judah have done nothing but what is evil in my eyes — from the very days of their youth. Israel especially has done nothing but provoke me with everything their hands built and offered. Yahweh declares it.
32.31: This city has been provoking my anger and my fury from the day it was built right down to today. Now I must remove it from before my face —
32.32: because of everything Israel and Judah have done to provoke me — their kings, their officials, their priests, their prophets, the men of Judah, the citizens of Jerusalem — every layer of the society, from top to bottom.
32.33: They turned their backs to me — refused to face me. Though I kept on teaching them, rising early to teach them again and again, they would not listen and would not take it in.
32.34: They erected their foul idols right inside the temple that bears my name — defiling the very place where I put my name.
32.35: They built Baal's high places in the Valley of Hinnom and made their own children pass through fire as offerings to Molech — something I never commanded, something that never crossed my mind to want. And through it all they were dragging Judah into sin.
32.36: Even so — this is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says about this city you are saying will fall to Babylon's king by sword, starvation, and plague:
32.37: I will gather them from every land where I drove them in my anger, my fury, my burning wrath. I will bring them back to this very place and let them live here in peace and security.
32.38: They will be my people. I will be their God.
32.39: I will give them a single heart and a single path — unified loyalty to me. They will revere me always, and it will be for their benefit and for their children's benefit long after them.
32.40: I will make a covenant with them that will never end — my commitment to bless them will never be withdrawn. And I will plant in their hearts a genuine reverence for me, so that they will never want to walk away from me.
32.41: I will take delight in blessing them and will plant them deeply in this land — faithfully, with all my heart, with all my soul, holding nothing back.
32.42: This is what Yahweh says: With the same certainty that I brought every one of these disasters on this people, I will bring every promised blessing I have declared over them.
32.43: Fields will be bought and sold in this very land — the land you are right now calling desolate, emptied of people and animals, handed over to the Chaldeans.
32.44: People will buy fields for silver, sign and seal documents, bring in witnesses — in Benjamin, around Jerusalem, in Judah's towns, in the hill country, the foothills, and the Negev. Because I will turn their captivity around. Yahweh declares it.

33.1: While Jeremiah was still shut up in the prison court, Yahweh's word came to him a second time:
33.2: This is what Yahweh says — the one who made this earth, who shaped it and set it firm. Yahweh is his name:
33.3: Call out to me and I will answer you. I will disclose to you great, unsearchable things — things you have never reached on your own.
33.4: This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says about the houses of Jerusalem and the palace buildings of Judah's kings — torn down to provide timber for the siege ramps, exposed to the sword:
33.5: The Chaldeans are advancing to fight, and they will pack those torn-down houses with the bodies of the men I have struck down in my fury — because I turned my face away from this city on account of all its evil.
33.6: But watch: I am bringing this city health and wholeness. I will heal my people and open up to them a full river of peace and faithfulness.
33.7: I will reverse the captivity of Judah and of Israel and build them back to what they were at the beginning.
33.8: I will wash them clean of every sin they committed against me. Every act of rebellion, every iniquity — I will forgive the whole account.
33.9: This city will become my trophy — a name of joy and praise and honor before every nation on earth. They will hear what I have done here, and they will tremble in awe at the overflowing goodness and prosperity I pour out on my people.
33.10: This is what Yahweh says: In this very place that you are now calling a wasteland — empty of people, empty of animals — in all the towns of Judah and Jerusalem's streets that lie silent and abandoned, there will once again be heard
33.11: the sound of joy and rejoicing, of bride and bridegroom singing, and the voices of those bringing thank offerings to Yahweh's temple, singing: 'Give thanks to Yahweh of hosts — for he is good! His steadfast love never ends!' I will turn this land's captivity around, just as it was at the beginning. Yahweh declares it.
33.12: Yahweh of hosts says this: In this land lying empty and silent — no person, no animal — in every last town, there will again be grazing grounds where shepherds bring their flocks to rest.
33.13: From the hill country towns to the Shephelah lowlands, from the Negeb to the land of Benjamin, from the villages around Jerusalem to every city of Judah — in all of them, flocks will once more file past the hands of the shepherd counting them. Yahweh declares it.
33.14: Days are coming — Yahweh declares it — when I will make good every word of promise I spoke over Israel and over Judah.
33.15: In those days, at that very time, I will cause a righteous Branch to shoot up from David's line — one who will govern with justice and righteousness throughout the land.
33.16: In those days Judah will be rescued and Jerusalem will live in safety. And the name this city will carry is: 'Yahweh himself is our righteousness.'
33.17: This is what Yahweh says: David's line will never go extinct — there will always be one from his descendants seated on Israel's throne,
33.18: and among the Levitical priests, there will always be someone standing in my presence — offering burnt offerings, presenting grain offerings, performing sacrifices — always, without interruption.
33.19: Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah again:
33.20: This is what Yahweh says: If you could somehow break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night — stop day and night from arriving on schedule —
33.21: — only then could my covenant with David my servant be broken, only then could his line be stripped of a son on the throne. And only then could my covenant with the Levitical priests who serve me be undone.
33.22: As the stars of heaven are uncountable and the sand of the sea cannot be measured out, so I will make the descendants of David my servant and the Levites who serve me beyond all reckoning.
33.23: Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah once more:
33.24: Have you paid attention to what people are saying? 'Yahweh has rejected the two families he chose.' That is how they have come to despise my people — treating Israel as if it were no longer a nation at all.
33.25: This is what Yahweh says: If I have not put in place my covenant with day and night — the fixed patterns of heaven and earth —
33.26: — only then would I cast off the descendants of Jacob and of David my servant, only then would I refuse to raise up rulers from his line over the offspring of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. But I will turn their captivity around. I will show them mercy. Yahweh's word.

34.1: This is the word Yahweh gave to Jeremiah when Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon — with his entire army, every kingdom under his command, every subject people — was waging war against Jerusalem and every town around it:
34.2: Yahweh, the God of Israel, says this: Go find Zedekiah king of Judah and deliver this word: Yahweh says — I am putting this city into the hands of Babylon's king. He will burn it to the ground.
34.3: You will not escape him. You will be captured, handed over to him. You will look Babylon's king in the eyes, and he will speak with you face to face. Then you will go to Babylon.
34.4: But hear one more word from Yahweh, King Zedekiah of Judah. This is what Yahweh says about you personally: You will not die by the sword.
34.5: You will die in peace. They will light burial spices for you, just as they did for the kings who came before you. And the people will mourn, crying: 'Oh, our lord!' I have spoken it. Yahweh's word.
34.6: Jeremiah the prophet delivered every one of these words to King Zedekiah in Jerusalem,
34.7: while Babylon's army was still hammering Jerusalem and the last towns of Judah — Lachish and Azekah — the only fortified cities left in all Judah that had not yet fallen.
34.8: This is the word Yahweh gave to Jeremiah after King Zedekiah made a covenant with all the people of Jerusalem to proclaim freedom for the slaves:
34.9: Every person was to release their Hebrew male and female slaves — no Jew was to be kept in bondage to another Jew.
34.10: All the officials and all the people who had made this covenant obeyed: they released their slaves, male and female, and no longer held them in servitude. They listened, and they did it.
34.11: But then they turned around. They went back and seized the slaves they had just freed — men and women both — and dragged them back into bondage.
34.12: Then Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah:
34.13: Yahweh, the God of Israel, says this: When I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, out of that house of slavery, I made a covenant with them. I said:
34.14: 'When a fellow Hebrew has served you for six years, you must release him at the end of seven.' But your ancestors would not listen to me. They tuned me out entirely.
34.15: You — just recently, you actually turned around. You did what is right in my sight: each of you proclaimed freedom for your neighbor. You made a covenant on this in the temple that bears my name.
34.16: But you turned back and desecrated my name. You went and retrieved every slave you had freed — men and women both — and dragged them back into servitude. You went back on your word in the house where my name lives.
34.17: Therefore this is what Yahweh says: You would not proclaim freedom — to your brothers, to your neighbors. So now I am proclaiming freedom over you — Yahweh declares it — freedom to meet the sword, the plague, and the famine. I will make you an object of horror to every kingdom on earth.
34.18: As for the men who broke my covenant and refused to keep the terms they agreed to in my presence — who cut a calf in two and walked between the pieces, calling down on themselves what happened to that animal if they ever broke faith —
34.19: the officials of Judah, the officials of Jerusalem, the court eunuchs, the priests, and all the people of the land who walked through that slaughtered calf —
34.20: I will hand them over to their enemies, to every faction that wants them dead. Their bodies will lie in the open — carrion for the birds of the air and the animals of the field.
34.21: Zedekiah king of Judah and his court — I will hand them to their enemies, to every faction that wants them dead, right into the hands of Babylon's army that pulled back from you only temporarily.
34.22: I am issuing the order — Yahweh declares it. I am turning Babylon's army around and pointing it back at this city. They will come, they will take it, they will burn it. Every city of Judah will be left empty and desolate.

35.1: This is the word Yahweh gave to Jeremiah in the days of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah:
35.2: Go to the Rechabite clan. Speak with them. Bring them into the temple, into one of the side rooms, and offer them wine to drink.
35.3: So I brought Jaazaniah son of Jeremiah son of Habaziniah, along with his brothers, all his sons, and the entire Rechabite household.
35.4: I brought them into Yahweh's temple, into the room belonging to the sons of Hanan son of Igdaliah, the man of God — the room beside the officials' chamber, above the room of Maaseiah son of Shallum, who kept the threshold.
35.5: I set out pitchers of wine and cups before them and said: Drink wine.
35.6: But they answered: We will not drink wine. Our ancestor Jonadab son of Rechab commanded us: You and your sons must never drink wine.
35.7: Build no house. Sow no field. Plant no vineyard — own none. Live in tents all your days. That way you will live long in whatever land you pass through as strangers.
35.8: And we have done exactly what our ancestor Jonadab son of Rechab commanded us. We drink no wine — not us, not our wives, not our sons, not our daughters.
35.9: We have built no houses, owned no vineyard, farmed no field, planted no crop.
35.10: We have lived in tents and kept every single command our ancestor Jonadab laid on us.
35.11: Then Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon invaded the land, and we said to each other: Come — let us get to Jerusalem. The Chaldean army and the Aramean forces are out there. That is why we are here in Jerusalem now.
35.12: Then Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah:
35.13: Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says this: Go tell the people of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem — Will you not take correction? Will you not listen to my words? Yahweh's word.
35.14: The command Jonadab son of Rechab gave his sons — never drink wine — is still being kept. Not one of them has touched it to this day. They obey a dead man's word. But I have spoken to you urgently, again and again — and you have not listened to me.
35.15: I have sent you my servants the prophets — every one of them, sent early and sent often — saying: Turn back, each of you, from your wicked path. Straighten your conduct. Stop chasing other gods. Do that, and you will remain in the land I gave you and your ancestors. But you would not listen. You would not even turn your head toward me.
35.16: The descendants of Jonadab son of Rechab have honored their ancestor's word. But this people — they will not obey me.
35.17: Therefore this is what Yahweh, the God of hosts, the God of Israel, says: I am bringing every disaster I pronounced against them down on Judah and on all who live in Jerusalem. I spoke — they did not listen. I called — they did not answer.
35.18: Then Jeremiah turned to the Rechabite household: Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says this: Because you obeyed your ancestor Jonadab's command, kept all his rules, and did everything he required —
35.19: therefore this is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: Jonadab son of Rechab will never lack a descendant who stands in my presence.

36.1: In the fourth year of King Jehoiakim son of Josiah of Judah, Yahweh gave this word to Jeremiah:
36.2: Take a scroll and write down every word I have spoken to you — against Israel, against Judah, against every nation — from the day I first spoke to you in Josiah's time right up to now.
36.3: Perhaps when Judah hears spelled out in full all the disaster I am planning for them, every person will turn back from their evil path — and I will forgive their guilt and their sin.
36.4: So Jeremiah called in Baruch son of Neriah, and Baruch wrote down everything Jeremiah dictated — every word Yahweh had given to Jeremiah.
36.5: Jeremiah gave Baruch his instructions: I am barred from entering Yahweh's temple.
36.6: So you go. On a fasting day, read from the scroll you wrote — Yahweh's words — to everyone in the temple. Read them also to all the people of Judah who come in from the towns.
36.7: Perhaps they will take their case before Yahweh and turn back from their evil paths — for the anger and fury Yahweh has pronounced against this people is immense.
36.8: Baruch son of Neriah did exactly as Jeremiah the prophet commanded: he went and read Yahweh's words from the scroll in Yahweh's house.
36.9: In the fifth year of King Jehoiakim son of Josiah of Judah, in the ninth month, a solemn fast was proclaimed before Yahweh — observed by all Jerusalem and everyone who had come in from the towns of Judah.
36.10: Baruch stood in the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan the royal secretary — in the upper court, at the entry to the New Gate of Yahweh's temple — and read Jeremiah's words from the scroll in the hearing of all the people.
36.11: When Micaiah son of Gemariah son of Shaphan heard every word of Yahweh from the scroll,
36.12: he went straight to the king's palace, to the secretary's chamber. The entire council was assembled: Elishama the royal secretary; Delaiah son of Shemaiah; Elnathan son of Achbor; Gemariah son of Shaphan; Zedekiah son of Hananiah — all the senior officials.
36.13: Micaiah told them everything he had heard — every word Baruch had read aloud to the crowds.
36.14: The officials sent Jehudi son of Nethaniah son of Shelemiah son of Cushi with a message for Baruch: Bring the scroll you read to the people and come here. Baruch son of Neriah picked up the scroll and came.
36.15: They said: Sit down. Read it to us. And Baruch read every word to them.
36.16: When they had heard every word, they looked at one another in genuine alarm. Then they said to Baruch: We have to take this to the king — every word of it.
36.17: Then they asked Baruch: Tell us — how did you write all this? Did Jeremiah dictate every word to you?
36.18: Baruch answered: Yes — he spoke every word to me, and I wrote it all down in ink on the scroll.
36.19: The officials said to Baruch: Get out. Hide — you and Jeremiah both. Do not let anyone know where you have gone.
36.20: They stored the scroll in Elishama the secretary's chamber, then went into the inner court and reported everything to the king.
36.21: The king sent Jehudi to get the scroll. Jehudi retrieved it from Elishama's chamber and read it aloud before the king and all the officials standing at his side.
36.22: It was the ninth month — midwinter. The king sat in his winter quarters with a fire burning in the fire pot before him.
36.23: Every three or four columns Jehudi read, the king took a scribe's knife, cut that section off, and threw it into the fire pot. Column by column, until the entire scroll was ash.
36.24: Not once did the king or any of his attendants flinch. Not one person tore their clothing. No one was afraid.
36.25: Elnathan, Delaiah, and Gemariah urged the king not to burn the scroll — but he ignored them.
36.26: Instead, the king ordered Jerahmeel — a prince of the court — along with Seraiah son of Azriel and Shelemiah son of Abdeel, to arrest Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But Yahweh had hidden them.
36.27: After the king burned the scroll — the one Baruch had written from Jeremiah's dictation — Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah:
36.28: Take another scroll. Write on it every word that was in the first scroll — the one Jehoiakim king of Judah burned.
36.29: And to Jehoiakim king of Judah, say this: Yahweh says — You burned that scroll. You asked: Why did he write that Babylon's king will come and devastate this land and wipe out every person and animal from it?
36.30: So this is what Yahweh says about Jehoiakim king of Judah: Not one of his line will sit on David's throne. His body will be flung out — exposed to the burning heat by day and the hard frost by night.
36.31: I will bring punishment on him, on his descendants, and on his court for their iniquity. Every disaster I pronounced against them — on them, on all Jerusalem, on all Judah — I will bring it. Because they would not listen.
36.32: Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to Baruch son of Neriah the scribe. Baruch wrote down at Jeremiah's dictation every word that had been in the scroll Jehoiakim king of Judah burned. And many more words of the same kind were added.

37.1: Zedekiah son of Josiah was installed as king in place of Coniah son of Jehoiakim — installed by Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, over the land of Judah.
37.2: But Zedekiah, his court, and the people of the land all refused to hear the words Yahweh was speaking through Jeremiah the prophet.
37.3: King Zedekiah sent Jehucal son of Shelemiah and Zephaniah the priest son of Maaseiah to Jeremiah with one request: Pray to Yahweh our God for us.
37.4: At this point Jeremiah still came and went freely among the people — no one had yet put him under arrest.
37.5: At that time Pharaoh's army had marched up from Egypt. When the Chaldean forces besieging Jerusalem got word of it, they lifted the siege and withdrew.
37.6: Then Yahweh's word came to Jeremiah the prophet:
37.7: This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says: Tell the king of Judah who sent you to ask me — Pharaoh's army that came out to rescue you is going to turn around and go home to Egypt.
37.8: And the Chaldeans will come back. They will attack this city, take it, and burn it to the ground.
37.9: This is what Yahweh says: Stop lying to yourselves — 'The Chaldeans are definitely leaving us.' They are not leaving.
37.10: Even if you managed to cut down the entire Chaldean army and nothing was left but wounded men lying in their tents — those wounded men would drag themselves up and burn this city down.
37.11: When the Chaldean army pulled back from Jerusalem because of Pharaoh's advance,
37.12: Jeremiah left Jerusalem and headed for the territory of Benjamin to take part in a property transaction among his family.
37.13: At the Benjamin Gate, a sentry named Irijah son of Shelemiah son of Hananiah seized Jeremiah and charged him: You are going over to the Chaldeans.
37.14: Jeremiah said: That is a lie — I am not going to the Chaldeans. But Irijah would not hear it. He arrested Jeremiah and handed him over to the court officials.
37.15: The officials were enraged. They had Jeremiah beaten and thrown into the house of Jonathan the scribe, which had been turned into a jail.
37.16: After Jeremiah had been dropped into the vaulted dungeon and had been there for days on end,
37.17: King Zedekiah sent for him and had him brought to the palace. Privately, behind closed doors, the king asked: Is there any word from Yahweh? Jeremiah said: There is. And then: You will be handed over to Babylon's king.
37.18: Jeremiah also pressed the king: What have I actually done to you, to your court, or to this people, that you have imprisoned me?
37.19: Where are those prophets of yours who promised you: 'Babylon's king will not come against you or this land'? Where are they now?
37.20: But now — my lord the king — please listen to me. One request: do not send me back to the house of Jonathan the scribe. I will die there.
37.21: Zedekiah gave the order, and Jeremiah was moved to the court of the guard — a measure of mercy. He received a daily ration of bread from the bakers' quarter for as long as the city's bread supply lasted. And there Jeremiah stayed.

38.1: Four of the king's officials — Shephatiah son of Mattan, Gedaliah son of Pashhur, Jucal son of Shelemiah, and Pashhur son of Malchiah — heard what Jeremiah was telling the people:
38.2: This is what Yahweh says: Whoever stays in this city will die — by sword, starvation, or plague. Whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans will live. His life will be his prize. He will survive.
38.3: This is what Yahweh says: This city will absolutely be handed over to Babylon's army. It will fall.
38.4: The officials told the king: This man must die. He is cutting the nerve of courage in every soldier left in this city, and in the whole population, by saying what he says. He is not working for the good of this people — he is working for their destruction.
38.5: King Zedekiah replied: He is in your hands. There is nothing the king can do to stop you.
38.6: So they took Jeremiah and lowered him by ropes into the cistern of Malchiah the king's son, which stood in the court of the guard. There was no water in the cistern — only mud. Jeremiah sank down into it.
38.7: But Ebed-melech, an Ethiopian who served in the royal palace, heard that they had thrown Jeremiah into the cistern. At that moment the king was sitting in session at the Benjamin Gate.
38.8: Ebed-melech left the palace and went straight to the king and said:
38.9: My lord the king — what these men have done to Jeremiah the prophet is wicked. They threw him into a cistern. He is going to die of starvation down there, because the city has run out of bread.
38.10: The king gave the order to Ebed-melech: Take thirty men with you and get Jeremiah out of that cistern before he dies.
38.11: Ebed-melech took the men and went to a storeroom under the palace treasury. He gathered old rags and worn-out garments and let them down by ropes to Jeremiah in the cistern.
38.12: Ebed-melech called down to Jeremiah: Pad your armpits with these rags and worn cloth before the ropes go under them. Jeremiah did it.
38.13: They hauled Jeremiah up out of the cistern. He remained in the court of the guard.
38.14: King Zedekiah sent for Jeremiah and received him at the third entrance to Yahweh's temple. He said: I have a question for you. Hide nothing.
38.15: Jeremiah answered: If I tell you the truth, you will have me killed. And if I give you counsel, you will not follow it.
38.16: So King Zedekiah swore to him in private: As Yahweh lives — the one who breathed this life into us — I will not kill you, and I will not put you in the hands of those who want you dead.
38.17: Then Jeremiah spoke: This is what Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel, says: If you go out and surrender to Babylon's officials, you will live. This city will not be burned. You and your household will come through.
38.18: But if you refuse to surrender, this city will be handed to the Chaldeans and they will burn it. You will not get out.
38.19: King Zedekiah said: I am terrified of the Judeans who have already defected to the Chaldeans. What if they hand me over to them and they brutalize me?
38.20: Jeremiah said: They will not hand you over. Trust Yahweh's voice — do what I am telling you. It will go well with you. You will come away alive.
38.21: But if you refuse, this is what Yahweh has shown me:
38.22: Every woman left in the palace of Judah's king will be paraded before Babylon's officials. And they will taunt you: Your closest allies deceived you and got the better of you. Your feet are sunk in the mire — and they have already turned and walked away.
38.23: All your wives, all your children — they will be taken by the Chaldeans. And you will not escape. Babylon's king will have you in his grip, and this city will burn.
38.24: Then Zedekiah said to Jeremiah: Tell no one about this conversation, and I will let you live.
38.25: If the officials find out that I talked with you and they come demanding: Tell us what passed between you and the king — every word, nothing held back, or we will kill you —
38.26: then you are to say: I was asking the king not to send me back to Jonathan's house, because I would die there.
38.27: Sure enough, all the officials came to Jeremiah and interrogated him. He answered them exactly as the king had told him, and they stopped pushing — no one had heard what was actually said.
38.28: Jeremiah stayed in the court of the guard until the day Jerusalem fell. He was still there when the city was taken.

39.1: In the ninth year of Zedekiah's reign over Judah, in the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon arrived with his whole army and encircled Jerusalem.
39.2: In the eleventh year of Zedekiah — the fourth month, the ninth day — the city wall was broken through.
39.3: All the senior commanders of Babylon's king came and took their positions at the middle gate — Nergalsharezer, Samgarnebo, Sarsekim the chief officer, Nergalsharezer the Rab-mag, and the rest of Babylon's command.
39.4: When Zedekiah and his soldiers saw them, they ran. Under cover of darkness they slipped out through the royal garden, through the gate between the two walls, and headed toward the Arabah.
39.5: But the Chaldean army ran them down. They caught Zedekiah on the plains of Jericho, seized him, and brought him to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah in the land of Hamath, where the king pronounced his sentence.
39.6: Before Zedekiah's own eyes, Nebuchadnezzar killed his sons at Riblah. Then he killed every noble of Judah.
